title: Design System: Nuxt UI (v4)
source: Primary refs: Nuxt UI docs on design system (Tailwind v4 tokens, semantic colors, runtime config), CSS variables (semantic color utilities + light/dark), and component docs (Button, Input, Form validation events, Modal, Toast), plus Nuxt UI Figma kit page. https://ui.nuxt.com/docs/getting-started/theme/design-system ; https://ui.nuxt.com/docs/getting-started/theme/css-variables ; https://ui.nuxt.com/docs/components ; https://ui.nuxt.com/docs/components/button ; https://ui.nuxt.com/docs/components/input ; https://ui.nuxt.com/docs/components/form ; https://ui.nuxt.com/docs/components/modal ; https://ui.nuxt.com/docs/components/toast ; https://ui.nuxt.com/figma
purpose: Reference knowledge for using the Nuxt UI v4 design system in design reviews and canvas mutation: component intent, semantic color usage, token-first theming, and consistent states (success, warning, error, disabled, loading). Intended to be paired with a Nuxt UI Figma kit/library and extended with component keys and org-specific rules.
scope: Applies to (1) design evaluation: validate that designs use Nuxt UI patterns, semantic colors, and states consistently; (2) design generation: guide selection of Nuxt UI components and tokens when producing screens; (3) theming: ensure token/variable-driven theming supports light/dark. Excludes assistant behavior, output formatting rules, and any project-specific implementation constraints unless provided separately. This KB does not include component keys; add them from the Nuxt UI Figma kit/library when available.
definitions: Nuxt UI uses Tailwind CSS v4 token customization via the @theme directive in CSS. Tokens can define fonts, colors, breakpoints, and other theme variables. (Nuxt UI design system docs)

A semantic color names intent/purpose rather than a specific value. Nuxt UI defines semantic aliases like primary, secondary, success, info, warning, error, neutral. These map to Tailwind palette colors or custom colors defined in @theme. (Nuxt UI design system docs)
Nuxt UI uses CSS variables as design tokens to support consistent theming with built-in light and dark mode behavior. (Nuxt UI CSS variables docs)
Nuxt UI provides utility classes like text-error and bg-success, backed by CSS variables (for light/dark). (Nuxt UI CSS variables docs)
Nuxt UI allows mapping semantic colors (e.g., primary, secondary, neutral) to theme colors at runtime via ui.colors (app.config.ts) and equivalent Vite config. (Nuxt UI design system docs)
Many Nuxt UI components expose a color prop that maps to semantic colors (e.g., UButton color="primary"). (Nuxt UI design system docs; Button docs)
Nuxt UI provides an official Figma design kit with components, variants, and local variables/tokens designed to match development props and tokens. (Nuxt UI Figma page)
Nuxt UI Form triggers validation when inputs emit input, change, or blur, and always validates on submit; validate-on controls timing. (Form component docs)
rulesConstraints: Prefer semantic colors over hard-coded values: use primary/secondary/success/info/warning/error/neutral to express intent. (Nuxt UI design system docs)
If defining custom colors via Tailwind theme variables, define the full shade range 50 through 950 for each color. (Nuxt UI design system docs)
Only map semantic colors to colors that exist in the theme (Tailwind default palette or custom colors defined via @theme). (Nuxt UI design system docs)
Nuxt UI semantic color utilities (e.g., text-primary, bg-error) are implemented via CSS variables that change between light and dark. Designs should validate both modes. (Nuxt UI CSS variables docs)
If a semantic color needs to be solid black/white, set it directly via CSS variables (not via a named palette color lacking shades). (Nuxt UI CSS variables docs)
Use component-level color props when supported (e.g., UButton color) to align design and development semantics. (Nuxt UI design system docs; Button docs)
For forms, expect validation to run on input/change/blur depending on validate-on and always on submit. Designs should include corresponding error messaging and states. (Form docs)
Modal content should be structured via slots (content, header/body/footer patterns). Designs should account for focus and dismiss behavior. (Modal docs)
Toast patterns may include actions; designs should reserve space for action affordances and avoid critical flows that rely solely on transient toasts. (Toast docs)
When using the Nuxt UI Figma kit, prefer using its variables/tokens rather than overriding styles per-instance; token edits should propagate across the system. (Nuxt UI Figma page)
If the Nuxt UI library is unavailable in a file, fall back to primitives but keep semantic intent (component intent + token roles). This is required for resilient canvas mutation.

do: Use semantic colors to communicate meaning (error for validation/destructive, warning for attention, success for confirmation, neutral for backgrounds/borders). (Nuxt UI design system docs)
Validate designs in both light and dark modes because Nuxt UI swaps semantic variable shades between modes. (Nuxt UI CSS variables docs)
Use component variants and props consistently with the system (e.g., Button as primary action; secondary actions use secondary/neutral and appropriate variant). (Button docs; design system docs)
For forms, show error states aligned to when validation occurs (input/change/blur/submit) and avoid surprising early error spam. (Form docs)
When generating designs from specs, prefer Nuxt UI components from the Figma kit and apply tokens via variables so global theming works. (Nuxt UI Figma page)
Document mapping from semantic intent to component choices (e.g., primary CTA -> Button primary; destructive -> Button color=error) to keep reviews consistent.
dont: Do not hard-code hex colors for semantic meaning when Nuxt UI semantic colors exist (unless explicitly required by a brand constraint). (Nuxt UI design system docs)
Do not define partial custom palettes (missing shades). Nuxt UI expects complete shade sets for custom colors. (Nuxt UI design system docs)

Do not map semantic colors to palette names that do not support multiple shades when configuration requires shades; use CSS variable direct assignment for solid black/white. (Nuxt UI CSS variables docs)
Do not rely on a single global error message when field-level errors are possible; align to validation triggers and provide recoverable guidance. (Form docs + general UX best practice)
Do not design critical confirmations only as toasts with no persistent acknowledgement when users may miss transient feedback. (Toast docs + general UX best practice)

examples: Use error for validation and destructive actions, warning for attention-needed states, success for confirmation states, and neutral for non-emphasis UI. (Nuxt UI design system docs)
Use semantic mapping for CTAs such as UButton color="primary" for main actions and error/warning where supported for destructive/attention states. (Nuxt UI design system docs; Button docs)
If validate-on includes blur, show field-level error after focus leaves the input; always validate on submit and show unresolved errors together. (Form docs)
Modal should have a clear trigger, and content should be placed in a dedicated content slot; header/body/footer sections can be used for consistent layout. (Modal docs)
Toasts can include actions (e.g., Undo). If included, design the toast to accommodate a button action without truncation. (Toast docs)
When using the Nuxt UI Figma kit, update local variables/tokens to theme the system rather than overriding local instances. (Nuxt UI Figma page)
edgeCases: If the Figma library is not enabled or not accessible, fall back to primitives while preserving semantic intent (component type + semantic color roles). Provide a user hint to enable the Nuxt UI library for full fidelity.
Custom colors must include all shades 50-950; missing shades can break consistent theming and semantic mappings. (Nuxt UI design system docs)
Semantic mapping to black/white should be done via direct CSS variable assignment (not via a palette name without shades). (Nuxt UI CSS variables docs)
If validation is configured to run on input, error copy may appear while the user is typing; prefer blur/submit for many inputs unless inline constraints are essential. (Form docs)
Because semantic variables can map to different shades in dark mode, validate contrast and readability in both modes. (Nuxt UI CSS variables docs)

To instantiate components via plugin automation from a shared library, you must capture the published component or component set keys from the Nuxt UI Figma kit/library. Node IDs are not portable across files; keys are used for importing components in plugins.
